app.title: Meeco SVX Guide
app.intro: A friendly, step-by-step guide to the Meeco Portal, the SVX Wallet and SVX Verify. Follow the path in order, or jump in anywhere you like.

ui.glossaryIntro: Plain-language definitions for every term used in this guide. Hover any underlined word elsewhere to see its meaning.
ui.draftNotice: Draft content: placeholder copy to be replaced with your final text.

portal.title: Portal
portal.blurb: Set up your space, company and apps. Do this first.

wallet-setup.title: Wallet setup
wallet-setup.blurb: Stand up the wallet before you use it.

tenant.title: Set up a Tenant
tenant.blurb: Your own private admin space in the Portal.
tenant.body: A tenant is your own private, walled-off area in the Meeco Portal. No credentials live here; it's purely for admin: managing your account and creating organisations. It's the very first thing you create.

steps.text: Setting up for the first time? Use the signup link to create your tenant.
After that, sign in to the Portal dashboard to manage it.
To set your tenant's name and logo, open the tenant logo menu → Manage Tenancy → Account Settings, then upload a logo and Save.

steps.alt: The Portal's Account Settings page under Manage Tenancy, showing the tenant name, the tenant ID, and a drag-and-drop area to upload the tenant logo, with Cancel and Save buttons.
steps.caption: Account Settings — update your tenant's name and logo

steps.text: To invite another tenant admin, go to Manage Tenancy → Administrators → Add Administrator.

steps.alt: The Add Administrator form under Manage Tenancy → Administrators, with fields for given name, family name, email address, and an optional welcome message, and an Add button.
steps.caption: Add Administrator — invite another tenant admin

organisation.title: Set up an Organisation
organisation.blurb: The team identity that controls wallet access.
organisation.body: An organisation lives inside your tenant. Like the tenant, no credentials live here, but it controls who can access the wallets you deploy from within the tenant.

steps.text: In the Portal sidebar, go to Organizations.
Click Add Organization, give it a name, an optional URL and a logo, then click Create.

steps.alt: The Add Organization form in the Portal, with fields for the organisation name and URL, a drag-and-drop area to upload a logo, and a Create button.
steps.caption: Add Organization — name, URL and logo

steps.text: Your new organisation appears in the Organizations list. Click its logo in the list to launch it.

steps.alt: The Portal's Organizations list showing an organisation row with its logo, name and date created; clicking the logo launches the organisation.
steps.caption: Organizations list — click an organisation's logo to launch it

steps.text: Inside the organisation, go to Manage Organisation → Administrators → Add administrator to create organisation-level admins.

steps.alt: The Add administrator form under Manage Organisation → Administrators for an organisation, with required given name, family name and email address fields, an optional welcome message, and an Add button.
steps.caption: Add administrator — create organisation-level admins

applications.title: Deploy a Wallet
applications.blurb: Stand up your organisation's credential hub.
applications.body: An application (or deployment) is a running service connected to your organisation. The simplest way to get one is an automatic wallet deployment via Deploy Wallet. This deployment is your organisation's Verified Credential hub, where you issue credentials, verify them, and configure SVX Verify for simple verification.

steps.text: Open your organisation's Applications and click Deploy Wallet.

steps.alt: The Devtools → Applications screen for an organisation with an empty applications list, showing the Deploy Wallet and Create application buttons.
steps.caption: Applications — click Deploy Wallet

steps.text: A View Wallet button appears, and a toast confirms your deployment request was successful.

steps.alt: The Applications screen showing a 'Successfully created a new deployment' toast, with a View Wallet button now shown in place of Deploy Wallet.
steps.caption: Deployment requested — success toast and the View Wallet button

steps.text: Click View Wallet to see the deployment status and information.
This usually only takes about 5–10 minutes.

steps.alt: The View application detail page showing an OFFLINE status badge alongside the deployment information — the Wallet URL, platform URL, tenant ID, organization ID and client credentials.
steps.caption: View Wallet — status OFFLINE while the deployment spins up

steps.text: Wait for the status to become online, refreshing the page to update it. Once online, your wallet is ready and its Wallet URL appears in the deployment information.

steps.alt: The View application detail page showing an ONLINE status badge, meaning the wallet deployment is ready, with its Wallet URL and deployment information.
steps.caption: View Wallet — status ONLINE and the wallet is ready

steps.text: Open the Wallet URL to enter your SVX Wallet dashboard, your hub for issuing and verifying credentials.

steps.alt: The SVX Wallet dashboard opened from the Wallet URL, with a sidebar (Credentials, SVX Verify, Wallet and Devtools) and an overview of sessions created, verified, pending and failed.
steps.caption: Your SVX Wallet dashboard — ready to issue and verify credentials

api-keys.title: Manage API Keys
api-keys.blurb: Secret keys so software can connect safely.
api-keys.body: API keys are secret passwords that let your software talk to the Wallet securely. Treat them like the keys to your house: keep them safe and never share them publicly.

steps.text: Open your Wallet dashboard and go to Wallet → API keys.

steps.alt: The API keys page in the SVX Wallet dashboard, with an empty list and a 'New API key' button.
steps.caption: The API keys page before you've added any keys

steps.text: Click New API key and give it a clear name, like 'Issuance pipeline', so you'll remember what it's for later.

steps.alt: The 'New API key' dialog with a Name field filled in, and a 'Create API key' button.
steps.caption: Naming a new API key

steps.text: The Wallet shows you a Client ID and a Client secret. Copy the secret now and store it somewhere safe.

steps.alt: The key-created screen showing the Client ID and Client secret, with copy buttons and a warning that the secret is shown only once.
steps.caption: Copy the client secret — it's only shown once

steps.text: You can only see the secret this one time. If you ever lose it, just revoke the key and create a new one.
Your new key now appears in the list, ready for your software to use.

steps.alt: The API keys list showing the newly created key with its name, client ID and creation date.
steps.caption: Your API key, ready to use

steps.text: Need to cut off access? Delete a key from the list to instantly revoke it; any system still using that key stops working straight away.

admin-accounts.title: Manage Admin Accounts
admin-accounts.blurb: Decide who can manage the Wallet.
admin-accounts.body: Admin accounts are the people who have access to your SVX Wallet. This is where you invite new people in and remove access when it's no longer needed.

steps.text: This page applies to standalone Wallets, where admins sign in with a passkey. If your Wallet is connected or deployed through SVX Platform, access is managed by the Platform instead, so this page doesn't apply.
Open Admin accounts to see everyone who currently has access to the Wallet.
To add someone, create an invite link and share it with them. They use the link to join and set up their own passkey sign-in.
To remove someone, delete their invite; they lose access to the Wallet right away.

wallet-settings.title: Configure Wallet Settings
wallet-settings.blurb: How your wallet behaves and protects keys.
wallet-settings.body: Wallet settings control how your Wallet behaves. They're grouped into tidy sections (System, Issuer, Verifier, Wallets and Bridge) so you can find the one you need.

steps.text: Open Wallet → Wallet Settings to see the sections down the left.

steps.alt: The Wallet Settings page, with a list of setting sections (System, Issuer, Verifier, Wallets, Bridge) on the left and the System settings open.
steps.caption: Wallet Settings, grouped into sections

steps.text: In SVX 4.0 the Wallet creates and looks after its own encryption keys (its KMS) for you, so there's very little to set up by hand.
Most teams can leave the defaults exactly as they are. Only change a setting if you have a specific reason to.
If you do change something, click Save to apply it.

schemas.title: Define Schemas
schemas.body: A schema is the blueprint for a credential. It lists the fields a credential must contain, for example 'full name' and 'date of birth'. Everything you issue starts from a schema.

steps.text: Open Credentials → Credential schemas, then click Create schema.

steps.alt: The Credential schemas page with an empty list and a 'Create schema' button.
steps.caption: The schemas page before you add one

steps.text: Save yourself time with a quick-start template: pick the Photo ID preset and the Digital Credentials (dc+sd-jwt) format, then give your schema a name.

steps.alt: The Create credential schema form with the Photo ID quick-start template and the dc+sd-jwt target format selected, showing the generated schema fields.
steps.caption: Starting from the Photo ID preset in the SD-JWT format

steps.text: SD-JWT is a privacy-friendly credential format. It lets the holder share only the fields they choose, rather than handing over the whole credential.
Glance over the fields the preset added (like name, date of birth, document number and portrait), then click Create schema.

steps.alt: The schemas list showing the new Photo ID schema with its ID and the dc+sd-jwt format.
steps.caption: Your Photo ID schema, ready to use

credential-templates.title: Build Credential Templates
credential-templates.blurb: Turn a schema into something you can issue.
credential-templates.body: A credential template turns a schema into a ready-to-issue credential. It adds how the credential looks and the rules for handing it out: a reusable recipe you'll use again and again.

steps.text: Open Credentials → Credential templates and click Create template.

steps.alt: The Credential templates page with an empty list and a 'Create template' button.
steps.caption: The templates page before you add one

steps.text: Give the template a name, then choose your Photo ID schema. The format is set for you to match the schema.

steps.alt: The Create credential template form with the Photo ID schema selected, styling options, and a live credential preview on the right.
steps.caption: Building a template from the schema, with a live preview

steps.text: Add a few finishing touches: colours, a logo, and how long the credential stays valid.
For SD-JWT credentials you'll also add a short credential type (VCT). It's just a label that says what kind of credential this is.
Click Create Template. It now appears in your list, ready to issue.

steps.alt: The credential templates list showing the new Photo ID template with its format and schema.
steps.caption: Your Photo ID template, ready to issue

issued-credentials.title: View Credentials
issued-credentials.blurb: The real credentials you've handed out.
issued-credentials.body: Issued credentials are the real credentials you've created and given to people (holders). This is where you can see, track and manage every credential you've issued.

steps.text: Open Credentials → View credentials to see everything you've issued.

steps.alt: The View credentials page listing an issued Photo ID credential with its format and issue date.
steps.caption: The issued credentials list

steps.text: Credentials are issued by your own API-connected systems, which create the credential from a template and hand it to the holder, so it lands in their wallet.
Setting up an API-connected system to issue credentials is out of scope of this guide.
Click any credential to see its details and track its status, and revoke it if it should no longer be trusted.

By default, the issuing wallet shows the list of issued credentials but not the claims inside each one. To view the claims, go to Wallet → Wallet Settings → Issuer and turn on "Persist issued credentials".

steps.alt: The Credential Details panel for an issued credential, showing its ID and format alongside the claims it contains — name, date of birth, document number and more.
steps.caption: A single credential's details

verification-templates.title: Create Verification Templates
verification-templates.blurb: Describe what you want to check.
verification-templates.body: A verification template is the question you ask: which credential a person should hold, and exactly which fields they need to show you. You set it up once and reuse it whenever you need to check someone.

steps.text: Open Credentials → Verification templates and click Create template.

steps.alt: The Verification templates page with an empty list and a 'Create template' button.
steps.caption: The verification templates page before you add one

steps.text: Switch to the JSON Editor, then pick a ready-made sample: choose 'DCQL - Photo ID (SD-JWT)' to ask for a Photo ID.

steps.alt: The verification template JSON editor with the 'DCQL - Photo ID (SD-JWT)' sample selected, showing the DCQL claims that will be requested.
steps.caption: Building the request with DCQL, using the Photo ID sample

steps.text: DCQL is simply the way the request writes down exactly which credential and fields to ask for. The sample fills it in for you, so there's nothing to write by hand.
Give the template a name and a short purpose, like 'Verify the holder's Photo ID', then click Create.

steps.alt: The verification templates list showing the new Photo-ID-check template.
steps.caption: Your verification template, ready to use

verification-submissions.title: Review Verification Submissions
verification-submissions.body: When you ask someone to prove something, their reply is a verification submission. Here you review what they shared and confirm whether it passed your checks.

steps.text: Open Credentials → Verification requests to see what's come back.

steps.alt: The Verification requests page listing a completed Photo-ID-check request.
steps.caption: The verification requests list

steps.text: Open a response to see exactly what the holder chose to share.

steps.alt: The Response Details panel showing a Verified status and the claims the holder shared — name, date of birth, document number, portrait and more.
steps.caption: A verified response, with the shared details

steps.text: The Wallet has already checked that the credential is genuine and hasn't been tampered with; the green 'Verified' badge means you can trust what you see.
Confirm whether the response passed your checks, and you're done.

svx-configuration.title: Configure SVX Verify
svx-configuration.body: SVX Verify is a ready-made, hosted way to request and verify people's data, with no app to build. Configuration is where you make it look and feel like your own.

steps.text: Open SVX Verify → Configuration. As you change things on the left, a live preview on the right shows exactly what people will see.

steps.alt: The SVX Verify Configuration page: branding fields on the left and a live preview of the verification screen on the right.
steps.caption: Branding, with a live preview of the verification screen

steps.text: Set your brand name, contact details, and links to your terms and privacy policy.

steps.alt: The contact information and legal-link fields in the SVX Verify configuration.
steps.caption: Contact details and legal links

steps.text: Choose which identity providers people can use, and whether they must accept your terms first.

There are two kinds. Wallet-based providers (like Meeco Wallet) let people share straight from a digital wallet, so they're simple to switch on and a great place to start. Account-based providers let people sign in with an existing account, but they connect through the Bridge and take a lot more to set up; in the list they're marked 'Bridge not configured' until that's done.
Account-based providers need the Bridge configured first, which is out of scope for this guide. Stick with a wallet-based provider like Meeco Wallet to get going quickly.

steps.alt: The identity providers list in SVX Verify, with the Meeco Wallet provider switched on.
steps.caption: Choosing which identity providers people can use

steps.text: Finally, pick which verification template SVX Verify is allowed to use (for example your Photo ID check), then click Save.

steps.alt: The presentation templates section with the Photo-ID-check template selected, and the Save button.
steps.caption: Linking your verification template, then saving

steps.text: Click Save & preview to spin up a real, live verification session; this is exactly what your users will see and use.
The preview on the right is only a rough guide and doesn't perfectly match the live result. Always confirm the real experience with Save & preview.

portal.label: Portal
portal.def: The Meeco Portal: the website where you set up and manage your tenant, organisation and applications.

tenant.label: Tenant
tenant.def: Your own private, isolated space on the Meeco platform where your data and settings live.

tenant-admin.label: Tenant admin
tenant-admin.def: A person who administers a tenant in the Portal, managing tenant settings and creating organisations. Tenant admins can invite other tenant admins.

organisation.label: Organisation
organisation.def: The company or team identity that issues and verifies credentials inside your tenant.

application.label: Application
application.def: A running service (like the Wallet) connected to your organisation. Sometimes called a deployment.

deployment.label: Deployment
deployment.def: A live instance of an application that you've switched on and connected.

wallet.label: Wallet
wallet.def: The SVX Wallet: one unified service that issues, holds and verifies credentials.

wallet-url.label: Wallet URL
wallet-url.def: The web address where your deployed Wallet becomes available once its deployment is online.

api-key.label: API key
api-key.def: A secret key that lets your software connect to the Wallet securely.

api.label: API
api.def: A way for one system to talk to another automatically: software to software, with no one clicking buttons.

admin-account.label: Admin account
admin-account.def: A login for a person who is allowed to manage the Wallet.

passkey.label: Passkey
passkey.def: A secure, passwordless way to sign in using your device (your fingerprint, face or PIN) instead of a password. In the Wallet, available in standalone mode.

kms.label: KMS
kms.def: Key Management Service: the secure vault that creates and looks after your encryption keys.

bridge.label: Bridge
bridge.def: A separate SVX Wallet deployment connected to account-based identity providers to help with verification.

verifiable-credential.label: Verifiable Credential
verifiable-credential.def: A tamper-proof digital claim about someone or something that others can check is genuine.

claim.label: Claim
claim.def: A single piece of information inside a credential, such as a name or date of birth.

schema.label: Schema
schema.def: The blueprint listing the fields a credential must contain.

sd-jwt.label: SD-JWT
sd-jwt.def: A privacy-friendly credential format that lets the holder share only the fields they choose, instead of the whole credential.

vct.label: VCT
vct.def: Verifiable Credential Type: a short label on an SD-JWT credential that says what kind of credential it is.

credential-template.label: Credential template
credential-template.def: A reusable recipe that turns a schema into an issuable credential, with display and rules.

issuer.label: Issuer
issuer.def: The party that creates and gives out credentials.

issued-credential.label: Issued credential
issued-credential.def: A credential that has actually been created and handed to a holder.

revocation.label: Revocation
revocation.def: Switching off a credential so it is no longer trusted, even though it was already issued.

holder.label: Holder
holder.def: The person or thing that receives and keeps a credential in their wallet.

verification-template.label: Verification template
verification-template.def: A definition of what information a verifier asks a holder to show.

dcql.label: DCQL
dcql.def: A simple way to write down exactly which credential, and which fields from it, a verifier is asking for.

verifier.label: Verifier
verifier.def: The party that requests and checks credentials.

presentation-request.label: Presentation request
presentation-request.def: The request a verifier sends asking a holder to share specific credential information.

presentation-response.label: Presentation response
presentation-response.def: The holder's reply containing the requested credential information.

svx-verify.label: SVX Verify
svx-verify.def: Meeco's hosted verification service for requesting and checking people's data, available through the platform.

svx-platform.label: SVX Platform
svx-platform.def: Meeco's wider platform that can host and connect your Wallet and manage who has access to it.

identity-provider.label: Identity provider
identity-provider.def: A service people use to share and prove their information, such as a digital wallet or an existing account.
